// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 10 — PROMPTS FOR TEACHING AND LEARNING

A graduate student studying for a qualifying exam types into Claude: "Explain the difference between classical and Bayes

Chapter 10 — Prompts for Teaching and Le — This is not Claude's failure. The explanation was accurate. The failure was in what the prompt asked for — an explanatio
NARRATION

A graduate student studying for a qualifying exam types into Claude: "Explain the difference between classical and Bayesian inference." Claude delivers four paragraphs. The student reads them, feels like she understands, and closes the tab. Three days later, on the exam, the question appears. She cannot answer it. This is not Claude's failure. The explanation was accurate. The failure was in what the prompt asked for , an explanation delivered to a passive reader , rather than what learning requires, which is active construction. Ambrose et al.

// SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.

·Write a tutoring prompt that requires you to produce an attempt before Claude responds
·Design a Socratic prompt that stages questions rather than delivering an explanation
·Build a misconception-check prompt that surfaces wrong beliefs and addresses them specifically
·Prompt Claude to give feedback on your reasoning rather than on your product
·Create a self-assessment loop that asks you to evaluate before Claude does
NARRATION

Write a tutoring prompt that requires you to produce an attempt before Claude responds Design a Socratic prompt that stages questions rather than delivering an explanation Build a misconception-check prompt that surfaces wrong beliefs and addresses them specifically Prompt Claude to give feedback on your reasoning rather than on your product Create a self-assessment loop that asks you to evaluate before Claude does The underlying design principle in each case is the same: cognitive labor is the mechanism of learning, and the prompt must protect that labor rather

// SLIDE 03 — CORE CONCEPTS

CORE CONCEPTS

The answer-first trap.→Active-constructive-interactive learning (Chi).→Staged hints.→Misconception diagnosis.→The Anthropic Learning Mode
NARRATION

The answer-first trap. Claude's default, when asked a learning question, is to answer it. That is appropriate when the goal is information retrieval. It is counterproductive when the goal is skill development or durable understanding. A prompt that asks "what is the difference between X and Y?" will produce a comparison. Whether the student learns from that comparison depends almost entirely on what they do with it , and the prompt as typically written does not require them to do anything. Active-constructive-interactive learning (Chi).

// SLIDE 04 — BEFORE / AFTER PROMPT WALKTHRO

BEFORE / AFTER PROMPT WALKTHROUGH.

·The prompt requires the learner to produce before Claude responds.
·It specifies feedback as specific rather than generic ("what I got right and wrong, specifically").
It stages the hint structurehints before full disclosure, not full disclosure first.
·It instructs Claude not to skip straight to the explanation.
NARRATION

Explain the difference between null hypothesis significance testing and Bayesian inference. Include examples. This is a reasonable question. Claude will produce a thorough answer. The learner will read it. Whether they learn depends on what they do next , and the prompt requires them to do nothing.

// SLIDE 05 — THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT YOU MUST

THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT YOU MUST VERIFY

Verify by closing the tab and explaining.→Verify misconception diagnoses against an authoritative source.→Verify staged hints are actually staged.→Academic integrity.
NARRATION

When using Claude for learning, the human gate is not just accuracy , it is whether you have actually understood. Verify by closing the tab and explaining. After any Claude-assisted study session, close the conversation and explain the concept to yourself or someone else without looking at Claude's output. If you cannot, you have not yet learned it. Claude's explanation was fluent; fluency is not understanding.

// SLIDE 06 — COMMON MISTAKES

COMMON MISTAKES

Asking for explanations instead of asking for questions.→Treating fluent output as evidence of understanding.→Not specifying that feedback should be specific.→Asking Claude to generate practice problems without a difficulty ramp.→Using Claude to avoid hard thinking.
NARRATION

Asking for explanations instead of asking for questions. "Explain X" is a passive trigger. "Ask me what I know about X" is an active one. The first delivers an artifact; the second starts a learning process. Treating fluent output as evidence of understanding. Claude's explanations sound right because Claude has been trained on texts that sound right. Sounding right is not a test of whether you understood. Test yourself.

// SLIDE 07 — BRIDGE

BRIDGE

Learning prompts protect cognitive labor by structuring what the learner must do before Claude responds. Handoff prompts

NARRATION

Learning prompts protect cognitive labor by structuring what the learner must do before Claude responds. Handoff prompts , the subject of the next chapter , protect a different kind of labor: the judgment that defines what an autonomous agent is allowed to do on your behalf. Both share the same discipline: a vague prompt produces an output you cannot supervise. A specified prompt produces an output you can check, correct, and reuse.

// SLIDE 08 — THESIS

THE CORE CLAIM.

Learning prompts protect cognitive labor by structuring what the learner must do before Claude responds. Handoff prompts

NARRATION

Learning prompts protect cognitive labor by structuring what the learner must do before Claude responds. Handoff prompts , the subject of the next chapter , protect a different kind of labor: the judgment that defines what an autonomous agent is allowed to do on your behalf. Both share the same discipline: a vague prompt produces an output you cannot supervise. A specified prompt produces an output you can check, correct, and reuse.
